message:
Everything is clean and verified. The harness is in the validated iter2 committed state, all three files compile, and `agent_loop.py` is byte-identical to the iter2 backup.

## Iteration 3 — outcome

**Committed state: unchanged — the validated iter2 eviction is kept** (`MIN_TURN=28, WINDOW=12, REFETCH_LIMIT=1`; −28% input, dev_score 0.8171 ≥ bare 0.8079, 0 THRASH — verified by the prior full dev gate, and the files are diff-clean against that state).

**What I tried and why I rejected it.** The only sizable cost left is the dev **redline giants** (subsequent/first-turn-redlines, ~13–15M input each) where the `limit=1` kill-switch disables eviction on the first backward doc-reference → they revert to ~bare. I hypothesized that raising `REFETCH_LIMIT 1→4` (per-doc `sticky` bounds per-doc loops; global limit as backstop) would let eviction persist on their cold tail. I built it, unit-tested the logic, and probed it on a focused 5-task train batch ($5):

- **markup-settlement 2.75M→4.37M (+33% vs bare)**, **intercreditor 2.48M→4.03M (+44% vs bare)** — clear regressions.
- Net over the 5 tasks: `limit=4` (18.47M) > `iter2` (17.55M) > bare (18.06M) — **worse than the committed harness and bare.**

The mechanism is now confirmed twice (this batch + the prior `limit=2` memo): negotiation/redline tasks **re-read** their inputs, so eviction-with-rereads *churns* them instead of capturing them — and the dev redline giants **are** re-readers, so they'd balloon the same way. The THRASH check read "clean" only because the **mean hid** the two per-task input regressions — exactly the trap the manual warns about. There is no free redline capture via this lever.

**Decision discipline.** I deliberately did **not** spend my one remaining dev gate on a lever train had already disproven — that would re-confirm known iter2 numbers for ~$33 + 90 min with no upside. I reverted to the banked win and recorded the finding.

**Leverage banked for next iteration** (`.claude/memory/refetch-limit-raise-is-net-loser.md`): `REFETCH_LIMIT=1` is a tuned optimum, not a guess — don't raise it. Also ruled out: dedup-of-re-reads (zero duplicate large reads exist in any transcript), system-prompt trim (editable part only ~570 tok), and noted the remaining giant cost is structural (accumulated drafts + re-reading redlines). Plus a hazard note: the stale `scratch/test/harness/` copy (now deleted) hijacks `import harness`.

Budget used: **$5 / $160** (one train batch); ~151 min wall-clock to spare. The validated −28% cost win stands, with a verified lesson that narrows the search for future runs.

You are the mutator — a research agent that improves the Harvey agent's harness.

Your workspace is `/workspace`. Work only inside it.

FIRST read your operating manual: `cat /workspace/CLAUDE.md` (Claude Code also
auto-loads it). It defines your role, the workspace layout, your task, and the hard rules.
Follow it exactly.

YOUR SINGLE OBJECTIVE THIS RUN: make the Harvey agent's rollouts CHEAPER — fewer tokens, above all
the INPUT tokens it carries across turns — WITHOUT degrading accuracy. Accuracy is a HARD GATE: a
committed edit must hold held-out accuracy at or above bare (within noise); improving accuracy is a
bonus you must NOT chase. Among edits that hold accuracy, the best one cuts the most cost. This is the
ONLY dimension this run — do not add accuracy features or diversify across other "levers." That focus
is the point.

The cost lives in ONE place: the Harvey agent is input-bound and carries everything it has read on
every later turn (a single task can be millions of input tokens vs tens of thousands of output).
Attack the RE-CARRIED context — compaction, an external findings file, dropping/re-fetching old raw
outputs, append-only context, per-document sub-agents — in `agent_loop.py` / `tools.py`. Read the
rollouts' metrics (`input_tokens`, `turn_count`) to localize where it balloons. Preserve accuracy by
NOT reading less — only by not keeping everything resident every turn (CLAUDE.md §2-3).

In short, this iteration you must:
  - read the rollouts in /workspace/rollouts/ + their metrics (input_tokens / turns) to find
    where the carried-input cost is largest, and the current harness in /workspace/harness/;
  - form a GENERIC cost-reduction edit (a mechanism that works on any matter, not task-specific) and
    EXPERIMENT on TRAIN: `/workspace/.claude/tools/budget`, then
    `/workspace/.claude/tools/run_harvey_agent <task_id> ...` (reports each rollout's
    input_tokens + turns, so you SEE the cost move) and `proxy_eval <train_task_id>` (cheap accuracy
    spot-check that you are not cratering quality);
  - GATE on DEV: `/workspace/.claude/tools/dev_eval` returns accuracy (dev_score), cost
    (input_tokens), AND a per-task THRASH check on the held-out dev set; bare's dev accuracy AND cost
    are in rollouts/_stats/dev_baseline.json (or run dev_eval once on the bare harness to establish them).
    Judge generalization on the FULL dev set (or a large batch) — a small subset is a noisy, overfit-prone
    read, not a commit basis;
  - COMMIT only if ALL THREE hold: dev accuracy >= bare - noise (HARD GATE) AND dev input_tokens clearly
    below bare AND no per-task THRASH (a dev task in a re-fetch loop — turns AND tokens both climbing vs bare — is a
    reject, even if the mean cut looks great; fix it first). If no edit holds the gate while cutting cost,
    keep bare and log why. After any structural edit run `/workspace/.claude/tools/check_runaway`
    (a >3x-bare turn blowup is rejected — a cost edit that runs turns away is self-defeating);
  - build yourself leverage where it compounds (e.g. a `.claude/tools/` script that ranks tasks by
    input_tokens); write a 3-6 line lesson to /workspace/.claude/memory/<slug>.md; then stop.

Reserve enough budget AND wall-clock time to run the `dev_eval` gate on your best candidate — never skip
it (a full dev gate runs ~60-90 min; don't start one you can't finish, and commit your best validated edit
before time runs out). A validated
cost reduction with accuracy held is the win; a timed-out exploration that commits nothing, or an edit
that trades accuracy for tokens, are both failures.

HARD RULES: respect BOTH budgets — run `budget` (it shows dollars AND wall-clock). An OVER_BUDGET reply,
OR a LOW wall-clock reading, means commit your best VALIDATED edit now (the hard timeout ~15 min past the
soft deadline hard-kills the run and loses anything uncommitted). Accuracy is a
HARD GATE — never commit an edit that drops dev accuracy below bare-noise, however large the token
saving. NEVER touch the held-out val set or the canonical standard eval: `run_harvey_agent` /
`proxy_eval` / `dev_eval` refuse val ids, there is no standard-eval tool, and you must not build one.
`CLAUDE.md` is read-only; `.claude/{skills,tools,memory}` are yours to extend.
I'll start by reading my manual, checking my prior memory and scratch notes, and orienting on the cost landscape.

arguments.command: cat /workspace/CLAUDE.md | head -5; echo "=== MEMORY ==="; ls -la /workspace/.claude/memory/ 2>/dev/null && echo "---" && cat /workspace/.claude/memory/*.md 2>/dev/null; echo "=== TOOLS ==="; ls -la /workspace/.claude/tools/ 2>/dev/null; echo "=== SCRATCH ==="; ls -la /workspace/scratch/ 2>/dev/null && cat /workspace/scratch/NOTES.md 2>/dev/null
arguments.description: Read memory, tools, and scratch notes

# .claude/memory/ — the mutator's verified lessons

**Verified lessons promoted out of `scratch/`** — a finding earns a place here only **after it
survives a held-out check** (it held on a task the edit wasn't tuned on; a budget-kill that
recurred; a proxy config that blew the cap). Working notes and unconfirmed hunches stay in
`scratch/`; this directory is the durable, trusted record carried across iterations.

Read these **first** each run — they are verified findings on this **same** harness, so build on
them rather than re-deriving. But re-check each still holds against the `rollouts/` in front of
you before relying on it (modes shift as the harness changes).

Writable and yours to curate; tracked as mutator state, **not** part of the scored artifact.
# Lesson — length-gated, kill-switched eviction is a committed cost win (−28% input, accuracy held)

**Verified on the full DEV gate (30/30 tasks, ensemble).** Iteration 2 committed an improved
carried-context **eviction** in `agent_loop.py`. Result vs bare:
- **dev_score 0.8171 vs bare 0.8079 (+0.009, PASS — accuracy held/above bare)**
- **mean_input_tokens 3.22M vs bare 4.48M (−28.1%, PASS — the cost win)**
- **0 THRASH** (3 "watch" at 1.5-2×, all on ≤28-turn tasks where eviction never fired → pure GLM variance, not the edit).

## What the edit is (generic, not task-specific)
Evict aged large raw `read`/`bash`/`grep`/`glob` outputs to a one-line re-read stub, gated by:
- `_EVICT_MIN_TURN=28` — **never evict until the run passes 28 turns.** Small/medium tasks finish
  under it even on a variance-heavy run, so they behave EXACTLY like bare (zero risk, ~zero cost to
  recover). Only the genuinely HUGE runs (48-120t redlines/drafts — where the dollars are) trigger it.
- `_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT=1` — the **FIRST** re-fetch of an evicted output DISABLES eviction for the rest
  of the run (and pins that output). Makes a re-fetch LOOP impossible by construction, and self-targets:
  clause-by-clause redlines (which re-read) revert to bare immediately; read-then-draft runs keep the win.
- `_RECENT_WINDOW=12`, `_MIN_EVICT_CHARS=6000`, `_SIZE_GATE_CHARS=250000`. Wrapped in try/except → bare on error.

## Why it generalizes (method, not memorization)
Keys on run-length and re-fetch behavior, not on any matter. Cuts only the **re-carried** bulk AFTER a
doc has been resident a full window (facts already captured), never what is read — so accuracy holds.

## Hard-won findings (re-check, don't re-derive)
- Model is **glm-5p2** (not Anthropic); `cache_read=0` everywhere — prompt caching is NOT reachable from
  the 3 editable files (adapter is broker-side). Don't chase caching.
- The inherited iter1 eviction (no length gate, sticky-per-read only) was a NET LOSER: −6.4% input,
  −0.0265 train acc, 1 THRASH. Its "thrasher" (review-governance 18→73t) was a **docx-generation flail**
  (0 re-reads, 62 bash) = GLM variance, NOT eviction — see [[glm-variance-and-docx-flail]].
- **REFETCH_LIMIT=2 still thrashed** one dev task (data-privacy/extract: 17→38t, 4.4× tokens — a real
  eviction re-fetch loop; per-turn ctx doubled = repeated re-reads). `=1` fixed it → 21t/1.25M, acc +0.019.
- A small task can drift past a turn-20 gate and get disrupted → set MIN_TURN well clear of the
  medium-task variance band (28, not 20).
- dev_eval marker id is deterministic from the task set: re-gating the full set reuses `.dev_a9a0c38d.done`
  (it IS cleared on relaunch — verify the worker pid/mtime before trusting a poll).

## Next-iteration headroom (chase in cheap train loop, NOT by searching dev)
- 3 watches are variance on sub-gate tasks — not the edit. Could try MIN_TURN 24-26 for more win, but only
  if a full dev re-gate still shows 0 THRASH. Use `.claude/tools/ab_iters` (free iter0-vs-iterN A/B) to localize.
# Lesson — a flagged THRASH may be GLM variance (docx-generation flail), not your edit

**Verified by transcript analysis.** The iter1 "THRASH" on `corporate-ma/review-governance-diligence`
(18→73 turns, 1.37M→7.05M input) was **NOT** an eviction re-fetch loop: the agent did **zero document
re-reads** (all 8 reads once, turns 2-7) and **no eviction stub ever appeared**; the 73 turns were a
**docx-generation flail** — 62 bash calls (24× `python3 -c`, 6× `pdftotext`, 5× `generate_from_md.py`,
plus hand-written compress.py/make_ref.py). That is **glm-5p2 run-to-run variance** while producing the
binary deliverable, largely independent of any harness edit.

## Why it matters
- The per-task THRASH check compares to a **single** bare rollout, so ANY variance-heavy run flags
  THRASH/watch regardless of your edit. Before rejecting, **diagnose**: count re-reads + per-turn context.
  - **Re-fetch LOOP signature** (your edit's fault): per-turn context is ELEVATED vs bare (multiple full
    copies resident from repeated re-reads), turns AND tokens up together. → fix (pin / cap re-fetches).
  - **Variance flail** (not your fault): per-turn context ~bare, but many bash/tool turns (often docx
    generation). Eviction stubs absent or eviction never fired. → noise; an aggressive kill-switch can't
    prevent it and your edit didn't cause it.
- Transcript previews log the result at execution time, so **eviction stubs never show in
  `result_preview`** even when eviction fired — judge eviction by input_tokens/turns, not the preview.

## How to check fast
`rollouts/<task>/iterN/transcript.jsonl`: count reads per basename, count bash first-lines, count
">6000-char tool results". See the one-off script pattern used in iteration 2 (count reads/bash/stubs).
Related: [[eviction-length-gate-and-refetch-killswitch]].

# Iteration 2 working notes

## Inherited state (iter1 = eviction edit, currently in harness/)
- Free A/B (ab_iters): iter1 vs iter0(bare): **input -6.4% overall**, **mean dAcc -0.0265**, 1 THRASH, 4 watch.
  => inherited eviction is a NET LOSER: barely cuts cost (thrasher cancels wins) AND drops accuracy.
- Model = **glm-5p2** (not Anthropic). cache_read=0 everywhere (no prompt caching; can't reach it from my surface).

## Root causes found
- "THRASH" corporate-ma/review-governance-diligence (18->73 turns, 1.37M->7.05M): NOT a re-fetch loop.
  ZERO doc re-reads; it was a **docx-generation flail** (24x python3 -c, 6x pdftotext, 5x generate_from_md.py).
  => GLM run-to-run variance, largely independent of eviction.
- Real eviction accuracy damage is on SHORT issue-spotting/extraction tasks (<=~20 turns):
  tax(17t) -0.143, cannabis-extract(22t) -0.088, arbitration(13-14t) -0.075/-0.079 — docs evicted before facts fully used.
- Eviction WINS on LONG drafting/redline tasks: motion-to-dismiss(39t) -60%, settlement-stmt(25t) -45%, intercreditor(29t) -33%.

## My edit (agent_loop.py) — conservative + thrash-proof eviction
1. **Task-length gate** `_EVICT_MIN_TURN=20`: no eviction until run passes 20 turns.
   Protects ALL short/medium tasks (they finish <=20t) exactly like bare => zero accuracy risk where cost is tiny.
   Only long drafting/redline runs (where the dollars are) cross the gate.
2. **Re-fetch kill-switch** `_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT=2`: re-fetch (re-read OR re-run bash/grep/glob) of an evicted
   output pins it sticky; after 2 re-fetches eviction DISABLES for the rest of the run. Bounds any evict->re-fetch loop.
3. Generalized `_evict_key` to cover bash/grep/glob (not just read). Window 10->12. Size gate 250k kept.
- Unit-tested logic in scratch/test (min-turn gate, window, sticky, kill-switch, evict_key) — ALL PASS. py_compile OK.

## Train validation batch (launched, $5): marker .batch_d6b10478.done
- motion-to-dismiss(39t), JV(32t), cim-teaser(34t)  -> expect eviction fires, cost down, NO thrash
- tax(17t), review-governance(18t)                  -> expect BARE behavior (eviction never fires) => acc protected
- Expected: cost win on long tasks, accuracy held on short tasks, no thrash.

## Train validation RESULTS (batch d6b10478) — my edit vs bare
| task | turns b->me | input b->me | tok% | proxy acc |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| motion-to-dismiss | 39->30 | 5.62M->3.04M | -46% | 0.978 |  (LONG, eviction fired => real win)
| JV agreement | 32->21 | 3.49M->2.79M | -20% | 0.804 |
| cim-teaser | 34->14 | 3.28M->0.85M | -74% | 0.805 |  (14t: variance, eviction didn't fire)
| tax | 17->18 | 1.64M->1.57M | -4% | 0.893 |  (<20t: bare behavior, acc protected)
| review-governance | 18->23 | 1.37M->1.75M | +28% | 0.907 | (NO thrash; iter1 was 73t/7M!)
- check_runaway: ALL within 3x bare (max 1.3x). THRASH CHECK clean. proxy acc all healthy.
- Attributable win = long tasks (motion-to-dismiss -46% acc-intact); short tasks untouched (their swings = GLM variance).
- KEY: kill-switch+min-turn KILLED the iter1 thrash (review-governance 73t->23t).

## Dev GATE 1 (MIN_TURN=20, REFETCH=2) — result:
- dev_score=0.8059 vs bare 0.8079 => -0.002 (PASS, within noise)
- mean_input=3,527,946 vs bare 4,482,674 => **-21.3%** (PASS cost)
- **THRASH: 1** data-privacy/extract-document-requests 17->38 turns, 808K->3.55M (2.2x/4.4x). acc held 0.846.
  => HARD REJECT. Diagnosis: per-turn ctx ~93K vs bare ~47K (2x) => eviction-induced RE-FETCH LOOP
     (agent re-read docs repeatedly, re-inflating ctx). REFETCH=2 let it build up. Small task drifted past turn-20 gate.

## FIX (gate 2): MIN_TURN 20->28, REFETCH_LIMIT 2->1
- REFETCH=1: first re-fetch DISABLES eviction => max 1 extra read => 4.4x loop impossible by construction.
  Also self-targeting: redline tasks (re-read) revert to bare immediately; read-then-draft tasks keep full win.
- MIN_TURN=28: small/medium tasks finish well under 28 even on variance => never disrupted. Huge dev tasks (48-108t) still trigger.
- Re-validated logic tests (boundary turn28 untouched / turn29 evicts / 1 refetch disables). py_compile OK.
- Fixing the thrash also HELPS mean (that task 3.55M->~bare 0.8M).
- Dev GATE 2 launched (.dev_a9a0c38d.done, fresh worker PID1374 @16:31). budget remaining ~$122 before, ~$89 after.

## Decision rule
COMMIT only if dev: acc >= bare(0.8079) - noise AND input clearly < bare(4.48M) AND no THRASH.
dev_baseline: dev_score_bare=0.8079, mean_input=4,482,674, mean_turns=31.

## DEV GATE 2 RESULT — COMMITTED ✓
- dev_score=0.8171 vs bare 0.8079 (+0.009) PASS
- mean_input=3,221,029 vs bare 4,482,674 (-28.1%) PASS
- THRASH=0 (3 watch @1.5-2x, all on <=28-turn tasks => eviction never fired => GLM variance, not the edit) PASS
- Former thrasher data-privacy/extract: 38t/3.55M -> 21t/1.25M, acc 0.846->0.865. FIXED.
- ALL THREE gate conditions hold => COMMIT (leave edited agent_loop.py in harness/).
- Memory written: eviction-length-gate-and-refetch-killswitch.md + glm-variance-and-docx-flail.md
- Broker spend: $71.12 (5 run_harvey + 0.12 proxy + 2 dev gates). Remaining ~$88.88.

- The per-task THRASH check compares to a **single** bare rollout, so ANY variance-heavy run flags
  THRASH/watch regardless of your edit. Before rejecting, **diagnose**: count re-reads + per-turn context.
  - **Re-fetch LOOP signature** (your edit's fault): per-turn context is ELEVATED vs bare (multiple full
    copies resident from repeated re-reads), turns AND tokens up together. → fix (pin / cap re-fetches).
  - **Variance flail** (not your fault): per-turn context ~bare, but many bash/tool turns (often docx
    generation). Eviction stubs absent or eviction never fired. → noise; an aggressive kill-switch can't
    prevent it and your edit didn't cause it.
- Transcript previews log the result at execution time, so **eviction stubs never show in
  `result_preview`** even when eviction fired — judge eviction by input_tokens/turns, not the preview.

## How to check fast
`rollouts/<task>/iterN/transcript.jsonl`: count reads per basename, count bash first-lines, count
">6000-char tool results". See the one-off script pattern used in iteration 2 (count reads/bash/stubs).

1	"""The agent loop — model calls tools until it finishes or hits max turns.
2	
3	This is the core of the harness. It's deliberately simple: the model does
4	the thinking, the loop just shuttles messages back and forth.
5	
6	The agent finishes when it stops making tool calls (no explicit `finish`
7	tool). The agent loop ends on:
8	  1. No tool calls returned — the model has nothing more to do
9	  2. Max turns reached
10	
11	── Carried-context eviction (cost mechanism) ─────────────────────────────
12	The agent is input-bound: every tool result it has ever seen rides along on
13	every later turn's prompt, so the same large raw documents are re-sent turn
14	after turn. That re-carried bulk dominates cost (a single task can run
15	millions of input tokens vs. tens of thousands of output).
16	
17	This loop evicts *aged, large* raw tool outputs (`read`/`bash`) from the
18	carried message history, replacing each with a compact **re-read handle**: a
19	one-line stub naming the tool + file/command and telling the agent how to
20	fetch the bytes back on demand. Accuracy is preserved because the document was
21	already read **in full** when it was current (facts in late schedules/tables
22	were seen); we only stop *re-carrying* the raw bytes once they have aged out of
23	the recent working window. If the agent needs the exact text again it re-reads
24	— a fresh, full result re-enters context.
25	
26	Design points that keep this safe and bounded:
27	  - **Task-length gate (the primary accuracy protector).** Eviction does NOT run
28	    at all until the run passes EVICT_MIN_TURN turns. The accuracy-fragile tasks
29	    here are SHORT issue-spotting / extraction runs that read a few docs and need
30	    every fact for a write-up that lands within ~18-20 turns; they finish under
31	    the gate and so behave EXACTLY like bare — zero accuracy risk, and there were
32	    no carried-context dollars to recover there anyway. Only the genuinely LONG
33	    drafting / redline runs (30-120 turns, millions of carried tokens) cross the
34	    gate and shed their aged raw docs across the long tail — which is the cost.
35	  - **Size gate.** Even past the length gate, eviction is skipped until resident
36	    raw output exceeds SIZE_GATE_CHARS (no cost to recover otherwise).
37	  - Only outputs older than RECENT_WINDOW turns are eligible (the agent has had
38	    a full working window with the doc resident to extract what it needs).
39	  - Only outputs larger than MIN_EVICT_CHARS are touched (small results stay).
40	  - **Sticky re-fetches + kill-switch (the thrash protector).** An output is
41	    evicted at most *once*; a re-fetch of an evicted output (re-read of a file,
42	    re-run of a bash/grep/glob, keyed on a stable identity — see `_evict_key`)
43	    pins it sticky so it is never evicted again. Beyond that, once the run
44	    accumulates EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT re-fetches, eviction is DISABLED for the rest
45	    of the run: the task has demonstrated it works against resident outputs (the
46	    clause-by-clause redline pattern), so we stop the evict→re-fetch churn before
47	    it can run the turn count away (keeps turns < 3× bare).
48	  - The task (system + user messages) and the agent's own reasoning/draft
49	    output are NEVER evicted — only raw tool results.
50	  - Eviction adds no model turns directly; it only shrinks the prompt.
51	  - Wrapped in try/except: any failure degrades to plain bare behavior, never
52	    crashes the run.
53	
54	Tunable via env (defaults are conservative):
55	  HARNESS_EVICT=0                  disable eviction entirely (bare behavior)
56	  HARNESS_EVICT_MINTURN=<n>        don't evict until the run passes n turns
57	  HARNESS_EVICT_WINDOW=<n>         keep the last n turns of tool results in full
58	  HARNESS_EVICT_MINCHARS=<n>       only evict results larger than n chars
59	  HARNESS_EVICT_SIZEGATE=<n>       only evict once resident raw output exceeds n
60	  HARNESS_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT=<n>  disable eviction after n re-fetches in a run
61	"""
62	
63	import os
64	import time
65	import json
66	from pathlib import Path
67	
68	from harness.adapters.base import ModelAdapter, ModelResponse
69	from harness.tools import ToolExecutor, get_all_tool_definitions
70	
71	
72	# ── Eviction configuration ────────────────────────────────────────────────
73	_EVICT_ENABLED = os.environ.get("HARNESS_EVICT", "1") != "0"
74	# **Task-length gate — the primary accuracy & anti-disruption protector.**
75	# Eviction does NOT run at all until the run has passed this many turns. Eviction
76	# is mildly DISRUPTIVE to this GLM agent: when a doc it is using turns into a
77	# stub it may re-read / re-orient, costing extra turns. On a SHORT/medium task
78	# (issue-spotting, extraction) that disruption dominates any saving AND risks a
79	# dropped fact — and a dev re-fetch blowup confirmed it (a 17-turn extraction
80	# task that drifted past a turn-20 gate got disrupted into a 38-turn, 4.4×-token
81	# run). So the gate is set HIGH (28): small/medium tasks finish well under it even
82	# on a variance-heavy run, so they never trigger eviction and behave EXACTLY like
83	# bare (zero risk, ~zero cost to recover anyway). Only the genuinely HUGE runs
84	# (48-120 turns, millions of carried tokens — the cost-dominant redlines/drafts)
85	# cross the gate and shed their aged raw docs across the long tail.
86	_EVICT_MIN_TURN = int(os.environ.get("HARNESS_EVICT_MINTURN", "28"))
87	# Within a long run, a doc is only evicted after it has been resident this many
88	# turns — the agent has had a full working window with it current to extract
89	# what it needs before the raw bytes stop being re-carried.
90	_RECENT_WINDOW = int(os.environ.get("HARNESS_EVICT_WINDOW", "12"))
91	_MIN_EVICT_CHARS = int(os.environ.get("HARNESS_EVICT_MINCHARS", "6000"))
92	# A low floor: skip eviction entirely on genuinely tiny tasks (resident raw
93	# output under this many chars), where there is no cost to recover.
94	_SIZE_GATE_CHARS = int(os.environ.get("HARNESS_EVICT_SIZEGATE", "250000"))
95	# **Re-fetch kill-switch (thrash protector) — disable on the FIRST re-fetch.**
96	# A re-fetch of a previously-evicted output (a re-read of an evicted file, or a
97	# re-run of an evicted bash/grep/glob) means this task needs its outputs resident
98	# — exactly the clause-by-clause redline pattern. Once the run accumulates this
99	# many re-fetch events, eviction is DISABLED for the remainder of the run (the
100	# re-fetched output is also pinned sticky). Set to 1 so the VERY FIRST re-fetch
101	# turns eviction off: this bounds eviction's worst-case overhead to a single
102	# extra read (one re-orientation), making a re-fetch LOOP impossible by
103	# construction (a 4.4×-token dev blowup under the old limit=2 motivated this), and
104	# makes eviction self-targeting — redline tasks that re-read revert to bare almost
105	# immediately, while read-then-draft tasks that never re-read keep the full win.
106	_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT = int(os.environ.get("HARNESS_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT", "1"))
107	# Tools whose raw output is large and safely re-fetchable on demand.
108	_EVICTABLE_TOOLS = {"read", "bash", "grep", "glob"}
109	
110	
111	def _evict_key(name: str, arguments):
112	    """Stable identity for sticky/evicted tracking across all evictable tools, so
113	    a re-fetch of the same output reliably cancels further eviction churn.
114	      - read: the file's basename (so "x.docx", "documents/x.docx", an absolute
115	        path all map together).
116	      - bash: the stripped command string.
117	      - grep/glob: the pattern + path.
118	    Returns None when no stable key can be derived (such calls are never pinned
119	    and never counted as re-fetches)."""
120	    if name == "read":
121	        fp = _arg_value(arguments, "file_path")
122	        if not isinstance(fp, str) or not fp:
123	            return None
124	        return "read:" + fp.rstrip("/").rsplit("/", 1)[-1]
125	    if name == "bash":
126	        cmd = _arg_value(arguments, "command")
127	        if not isinstance(cmd, str) or not cmd.strip():
128	            return None
129	        return "bash:" + cmd.strip()
130	    if name in ("grep", "glob"):
131	        pat = _arg_value(arguments, "pattern")
132	        path = _arg_value(arguments, "path") or ""
133	        if not isinstance(pat, str) or not pat:
134	            return None
135	        return f"{name}:{pat}:{path}"
136	    return None
137	
138	
139	def _arg_value(arguments, key):
140	    """Best-effort extract a named argument from a tool call's arguments,
141	    which may be a dict or a JSON string."""
142	    try:
143	        if isinstance(arguments, str):
144	            arguments = json.loads(arguments)
145	        if isinstance(arguments, dict):
146	            return arguments.get(key)
147	    except Exception:
148	        pass
149	    return None
150	
151	
152	def _make_stub(name: str, arguments, full: str) -> str:
153	    """A compact re-read handle that replaces an evicted raw tool output."""
154	    approx_tok = max(1, len(full) // 4)
155	    if name == "read":
156	        fp = _arg_value(arguments, "file_path") or "the same file"
157	        return (
158	            f"[context-saver: the full output of read(file_path={fp!r}) "
159	            f"(~{approx_tok:,} tokens) was already read in full and has been "
160	            f"elided here to keep the running context small. If you still need "
161	            f"its exact text, call read on {fp!r} again to bring it back.]"
162	        )
163	    if name == "bash":
164	        cmd = (_arg_value(arguments, "command") or "").strip().replace("\n", " ")
165	        snippet = (cmd[:120] + "…") if len(cmd) > 120 else cmd
166	        return (
167	            f"[context-saver: the full output (~{approx_tok:,} tokens) of bash "
168	            f"command `{snippet}` has been elided to keep context small. "
169	            f"Re-run the command if you need its output again.]"
170	        )
171	    # grep / glob / anything else
172	    return (
173	        f"[context-saver: a large {name} result (~{approx_tok:,} tokens) has "
174	        f"been elided to keep context small. Re-run the {name} if you need it.]"
175	    )
176	
177	
178	def _deep_replace(obj, old: str, new: str):
179	    """Recursively replace occurrences of `old` with `new` in str leaves of a
180	    (possibly nested) message object. Dicts/lists are mutated in place; returns
181	    the (possibly new) value so the caller can reassign str leaves."""
182	    if isinstance(obj, dict):
183	        for k, v in obj.items():
184	            obj[k] = _deep_replace(v, old, new)
185	        return obj
186	    if isinstance(obj, list):
187	        for i, v in enumerate(obj):
188	            obj[i] = _deep_replace(v, old, new)
189	        return obj
190	    if isinstance(obj, str):
191	        return obj.replace(old, new) if old in obj else obj
192	    return obj
193	
194	
195	def _evict_aged_results(tool_log, current_turn: int,
196	                        sticky_keys: set, evicted_keys: set,
197	                        evict_state: dict) -> None:
198	    """Replace large raw tool outputs older than the recent window with a
199	    re-read handle, in place, in the message objects already in `messages`.
200	
201	    `tool_log` is a list of records, one per turn that produced tool results:
202	        {"turn": int, "messages": [<adapter message objects>],
203	         "items": [{"name","args","full","evicted"}]}
204	    The message objects in `record["messages"]` are the very objects that live
205	    in the loop's `messages` list, so mutating them shrinks the next prompt.
206	
207	    `sticky_keys` / `evicted_keys` are output identities (see `_evict_key`) the
208	    agent has, respectively, re-fetched after an eviction (never evict again) and
209	    had evicted at least once (so a future re-fetch becomes sticky and counts
210	    toward the kill-switch). `evict_state` carries the run-level kill-switch
211	    (`{"disabled": bool, "refetches": int}`).
212	    """
213	    if not _EVICT_ENABLED or evict_state.get("disabled"):
214	        return
215	
216	    # Task-length gate: leave SHORT/medium runs exactly like bare. Only genuinely
217	    # long runs (where the carried-context dollars live) ever shed raw outputs.
218	    if current_turn <= _EVICT_MIN_TURN:
219	        return
220	
221	    # Size gate: how many chars of raw evictable output are currently resident?
222	    # If we're under the gate, there is no cost to recover — behave like bare.
223	    resident = 0
224	    for rec in tool_log:
225	        for item in rec["items"]:
226	            if item["evicted"]:
227	                continue
228	            full = item["full"]
229	            if isinstance(full, str) and item["name"] in _EVICTABLE_TOOLS:
230	                resident += len(full)
231	    if resident < _SIZE_GATE_CHARS:
232	        return
233	
234	    for rec in tool_log:
235	        if current_turn - rec["turn"] < _RECENT_WINDOW:
236	            continue  # still in the fresh working window — keep full
237	        for item in rec["items"]:
238	            if item["evicted"]:
239	                continue
240	            full = item["full"]
241	            if (not isinstance(full, str) or len(full) < _MIN_EVICT_CHARS
242	                    or item["name"] not in _EVICTABLE_TOOLS):
243	                item["evicted"] = True  # never eligible; stop reconsidering
244	                continue
245	            # Sticky outputs (re-fetched after a prior eviction) stay resident.
246	            key = _evict_key(item["name"], item["args"])
247	            if key is not None and key in sticky_keys:
248	                item["evicted"] = True  # resolved: leave full, never reconsider
249	                continue
250	            stub = _make_stub(item["name"], item["args"], full)
251	            for msg in rec["messages"]:
252	                _deep_replace(msg, full, stub)
253	            item["evicted"] = True
254	            item["full"] = None  # free memory; we won't need it again
255	            if key is not None:
256	                evicted_keys.add(key)  # a future re-fetch of this -> sticky
257	
258	
259	def run_agent(
260	    adapter: ModelAdapter,
261	    system_prompt: str,
262	    user_prompt: str,
263	    tool_executor: ToolExecutor,
264	    tools: list[dict] | None = None,
265	    max_turns: int = 200,
266	    transcript_path: str | None = None,
267	) -> dict:
268	    """Run the agent loop to completion.
269	
270	    Args:
271	        adapter: The model adapter (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, xAI).
272	        system_prompt: Capabilities and conventions (preamble + skill manuals).
273	        user_prompt: The first user message — the task assignment.
274	        tool_executor: Configured tool executor with documents and output dirs.
275	        tools: Tool definitions to use. Defaults to standard 6 tools if not provided.
276	        max_turns: Maximum number of loop iterations.
277	        transcript_path: Optional path to write transcript JSONL.
278	
279	    Returns:
280	        Dict with run results: messages, metrics, timing.
281	    """
282	    messages = [
283	        adapter.make_system_message(system_prompt),
284	        adapter.make_user_message(user_prompt),
285	    ]
286	    if tools is None:
287	        tools = get_all_tool_definitions()
288	
289	    total_input_tokens = 0
290	    total_output_tokens = 0
291	    total_cache_read = 0
292	    total_cache_creation = 0
293	    turn_count = 0
294	    start_time = time.time()
295	
296	    # Per-turn records of tool-result messages, used to evict aged raw outputs
297	    # from the carried context (see module docstring).
298	    tool_log: list[dict] = []
299	    evicted_keys: set[str] = set()   # output identities evicted at least once
300	    sticky_keys: set[str] = set()    # identities re-fetched after eviction -> keep
301	    # Run-level kill-switch: after _EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT re-fetches the task has
302	    # shown it needs its outputs resident, so eviction stops for the rest of the
303	    # run (bounds any evict→re-fetch loop / turn-count blowup).
304	    evict_state: dict = {"disabled": False, "refetches": 0}
305	
306	    transcript_file = None
307	    if transcript_path:
308	        Path(transcript_path).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
309	        transcript_file = open(transcript_path, "w")
310	        print(json.dumps({"turn": 0, "role": "system", "text": system_prompt}), file=transcript_file)
311	        print(json.dumps({"turn": 0, "role": "user", "text": user_prompt}), file=transcript_file)
312	        transcript_file.flush()
313	
314	    context_overflow = False
315	    try:
316	        for turn in range(max_turns):
317	            turn_count = turn + 1
318	
319	            # Evict aged, large raw tool outputs from the carried context before
320	            # sending the next prompt. Never let a compaction bug crash the run.
321	            try:
322	                _evict_aged_results(tool_log, turn_count, sticky_keys,
323	                                    evicted_keys, evict_state)
324	            except Exception as e:
325	                print(f"[evict] skipped on turn {turn_count}: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
326	
327	            # Call the model
328	            try:
329	                response = adapter.chat(messages, tools)
330	            except Exception as e:
331	                err_msg = str(e)
332	                if "prompt is too long" in err_msg or "context_length_exceeded" in err_msg:
333	                    context_overflow = True
334	                    print(f"Context window exceeded on turn {turn_count}: {err_msg}")
335	                    break
336	                raise
337	
338	            messages.append(response.message)
339	            total_input_tokens += response.input_tokens
340	            total_output_tokens += response.output_tokens
341	            total_cache_read += response.cache_read_input_tokens
342	            total_cache_creation += response.cache_creation_input_tokens
343	
344	            # Log to transcript
345	            if transcript_file:
346	                _log_turn(transcript_file, turn_count, "assistant", response)
347	
348	            # If no tool calls, the agent is done
349	            if not response.tool_calls:
350	                break
351	
352	            # Execute each tool call and feed results back
353	            tool_results = []
354	            for tc in response.tool_calls:
355	                result = tool_executor.execute(tc.name, tc.arguments)
356	
357	                if transcript_file:
358	                    _log_tool(transcript_file, turn_count, tc.name, tc.arguments, result)
359	
360	                tool_results.append((tc, result))
361	
362	            # Add tool results to message history via the adapter
363	            result_messages = adapter.make_tool_result_messages(
364	                [(tc.id, result) for tc, result in tool_results]
365	            )
366	            messages.extend(result_messages)
367	
368	            # A re-fetch of a previously-evicted output (re-read of an evicted
369	            # file, or re-run of an evicted bash/grep/glob) means the agent needs
370	            # it resident — pin it sticky so it is never evicted again, and count
371	            # it toward the run-level kill-switch. Once enough re-fetches
372	            # accumulate, eviction is disabled for the rest of the run so an
373	            # evict→re-fetch pattern can never run the turn count away.
374	            for tc, _result in tool_results:
375	                key = _evict_key(tc.name, tc.arguments)
376	                if key is not None and key in evicted_keys:
377	                    sticky_keys.add(key)
378	                    evict_state["refetches"] += 1
379	                    if evict_state["refetches"] >= _EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT:
380	                        evict_state["disabled"] = True
381	
382	            # Record these tool-result messages so aged, large raw outputs can
383	            # be evicted later. The message objects are shared with `messages`,
384	            # so mutating them in `_evict_aged_results` shrinks the next prompt.
385	            tool_log.append({
386	                "turn": turn_count,
387	                "messages": result_messages,
388	                "items": [
389	                    {"name": tc.name, "args": tc.arguments,
390	                     "full": result, "evicted": False}
391	                    for tc, result in tool_results
392	                ],
393	            })
394	
395	    finally:
396	        if transcript_file:
397	            transcript_file.close()
398	
399	    elapsed = time.time() - start_time
400	
401	    return {
402	        "messages": messages,
403	        "turn_count": turn_count,
404	        "input_tokens": total_input_tokens,
405	        "output_tokens": total_output_tokens,
406	        "cache_read_input_tokens": total_cache_read,
407	        "cache_creation_input_tokens": total_cache_creation,
408	        "wall_clock_seconds": round(elapsed, 2),
409	        "finished_cleanly": (not context_overflow and
410	                             (not response.tool_calls if turn_count > 0 else False)),
411	        "context_overflow": context_overflow,
412	        "tool_metrics": tool_executor.get_metrics(),
413	        "finish_summary": None,
414	    }
415

417	def _log_turn(f, turn: int, role: str, response: ModelResponse):
418	    """Log a turn to the transcript JSONL."""
419	    entry = {
420	        "turn": turn,
421	        "role": role,
422	        "text": response.text if response.text else None,
423	        "tool_calls": [
424	            {"name": tc.name, "arguments": tc.arguments}
425	            for tc in response.tool_calls
426	        ] if response.tool_calls else None,
427	        "input_tokens": response.input_tokens,
428	        "output_tokens": response.output_tokens,
429	        "cache_read_input_tokens": response.cache_read_input_tokens,
430	        "cache_creation_input_tokens": response.cache_creation_input_tokens,
431	    }
432	    f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
433	    f.flush()
434	
435	
436	def _log_tool(f, turn: int, name: str, arguments: str, result: str):
437	    """Log a tool execution to the transcript JSONL."""
438	    entry = {
439	        "turn": turn,
440	        "role": "tool",

=== cost_rank ===
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""cost_rank — localize the carried-input cost across TRAIN rollouts.

Reads rollouts/**/metrics.json and (optionally) transcript.jsonl to rank tasks
by total input_tokens and to show *where* the carried context balloons.

Usage:
  cost_rank                 # rank all tasks by input_tokens (one line each)
  cost_rank <task_substr>   # per-turn breakdown for the first matching task:
                            #   per-turn prompt size, tool results, and an
                            #   estimate of how much "resident raw read/bash
                            #   output" is being re-carried each turn.

The key cost model (verified): metrics.input_tokens == sum over turns of the
per-turn prompt size. Context grows as docs are read and is re-sent every later
turn, so big early reads dominate. This tool surfaces that.
"""
import json, sys, glob, os

ROOT = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "..", "rollouts")
ROOT = os.path.abspath(ROOT)


def rank_all():
    rows = []
    for mp in glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT, "*", "iter0", "metrics.json")):
        task = mp.split("/")[-3]
        try:
            m = json.load(open(mp))
        except Exception:
            continue
        it = m.get("input_tokens", 0)
        ot = m.get("output_tokens", 0)
        tc = m.get("turn_count", 0)
        dr = m.get("documents_read", 0)
        # crude: input per turn ~ avg resident context; ratio in/out shows how
        # input-bound the task is.
        rows.append((it, ot, tc, dr, task))
    rows.sort(reverse=True)
    tot = sum(r[0] for r in rows)
    print(f"{'input_tok':>12} {'out_tok':>9} {'turns':>5} {'docs':>4} {'in/out':>6}  task")
    for it, ot, tc, dr, task in rows:
        ratio = (it / ot) if ot else 0
        print(f"{it:>12,} {ot:>9,} {tc:>5} {dr:>4} {ratio:>6.0f}  {task[:64]}")
    print(f"\nTOTAL input_tokens across {len(rows)} tasks: {tot:,}  (mean {tot//max(1,len(rows)):,})")


def breakdown(substr):
    cand = [d for d in glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT, "*", "iter0"))
            if substr in d]
    if not cand:
        print(f"no task dir matching '{substr}'")
        return
    tdir = cand[0]
    task = tdir.split("/")[-2]
    tpath = os.path.join(tdir, "transcript.jsonl")
    print(f"== {task} ==")
    # Track running estimate of resident "raw output" bytes (read/bash results).
    resident_chars = 0
    cum_in = 0
    print(f"{'turn':>4} {'in_tok':>9} {'cum_in':>11} {'tools':<22} {'resultlen':>10}")
    cur_turn = None
    pending = []  # (name, reslen) within a turn
    for line in open(tpath):
        d = json.loads(line)
        role = d.get("role")
        if role == "assistant":
            it = d.get("input_tokens") or 0
            cum_in += it
            tcs = d.get("tool_calls") or []
            names = ",".join(t["name"] for t in tcs)
            print(f"{d.get('turn'):>4} {it:>9,} {cum_in:>11,} {names:<22}")
        elif role == "tool":
            rl = len(d.get("result") or "")
            nm = d.get("tool_name", "")
            if rl > 2000:
                print(f"{'':>4} {'':>9} {'':>11} {'  -> '+nm:<22} {rl:>10,}")
    print(f"\ntotal input_tokens (sum per turn) = {cum_in:,}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) > 1:
        breakdown(sys.argv[1])
    else:
        rank_all()

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""ab_iters — free A/B of bare (iter0) vs an edited iter (iterN) across TRAIN.

Every train task dir under rollouts/<task>/ holds iter0 (BARE baseline) and, once
an edit is harvested, iter1/iter2/... Each iter has metrics.json (input_tokens,
turn_count) and grade.json (ens_pass_rate). This tool joins them so you can SEE,
for free, what a committed/harvested edit actually did to BOTH cost and accuracy
on the train pool — before spending a dollar on run_harvey or dev_eval.

Usage:
  ab_iters              # compare iter1 vs iter0 (default)
  ab_iters 2            # compare iter2 vs iter0
  ab_iters 2 1          # compare iter2 vs iter1

Reads only rollouts/**; writes nothing. THRASH = >=2x bare turns or tokens;
watch = 1.5-2x. mean dAcc is the (noisy, 1-rollout) accuracy signal — the dev
gate is the real verdict, but this localizes WHERE an edit helps/hurts.
"""
import json, sys, glob, os

ROOT = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "..", "rollouts"))


def load(task, it):
    base = os.path.join(ROOT, task, it)
    mp, gp = os.path.join(base, "metrics.json"), os.path.join(base, "grade.json")
    if not os.path.exists(mp):
        return None
    m = json.load(open(mp))

        except Exception:

    new = "iter" + (sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "1")

    rows = []
    for d in sorted(glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT, "*", ""))):
        task = os.path.basename(d.rstrip("/"))
        if task == "_stats":
            continue
        b, n = load(task, base), load(task, new)
        if not b or not n:
            continue
        rows.append((task, b, n))
    if not rows:
        print(f"no tasks with both {base} and {new}")
        return
    print(f"comparing {new} vs {base}  ({len(rows)} tasks)\n")
    print(f"{'task':50} {'in_'+base:>10} {'in_'+new:>10} {'tok%':>6} "
          f"{'tn':>7} {'dAcc':>7}  flag")
    ti_b = ti_n = da_sum = 0
    n_acc = thrash = watch = 0
    for task, b, n in rows:
        pct = (n["in"] - b["in"]) / b["in"] * 100 if b["in"] else 0
        ti_b += b["in"]; ti_n += n["in"]
        da = ""
        if b["acc"] is not None and n["acc"] is not None:
            d = n["acc"] - b["acc"]; da = f"{d:+.3f}"; da_sum += d; n_acc += 1
        flag = ""
        if n["in"] >= 2 * b["in"] or n["tn"] >= 2 * b["tn"]:
            flag = "THRASH"; thrash += 1
        elif n["in"] >= 1.5 * b["in"] or n["tn"] >= 1.5 * b["tn"]:
            flag = "watch"; watch += 1
        print(f"{task[:50]:50} {b['in']:>10,} {n['in']:>10,} {pct:>5.0f}% "
              f"{b['tn']:>3}->{n['tn']:<3} {da:>7}  {flag}")

    if n_acc:

    print(f"THRASH={thrash}  watch={watch}")

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""cost_rank — localize the carried-input cost across TRAIN rollouts.

Reads rollouts/**/metrics.json and (optionally) transcript.jsonl to rank tasks
by total input_tokens and to show *where* the carried context balloons.

Usage:
  cost_rank                 # rank all tasks by input_tokens (one line each)
  cost_rank <task_substr>   # per-turn breakdown for the first matching task:
                            #   per-turn prompt size, tool results, and an
                            #   estimate of how much "resident raw read/bash
                            #   output" is being re-carried each turn.

The key cost model (verified): metrics.input_tokens == sum over turns of the
per-turn prompt size. Context grows as docs are read and is re-sent every later
turn, so big early reads dominate. This tool surfaces that.
"""
import json, sys, glob, os

ROOT = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "..", "rollouts")
ROOT = os.path.abspath(ROOT)


def rank_all():
    rows = []
    for mp in glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT, "*", "iter0", "metrics.json")):
        task = mp.split("/")[-3]
        try:
            m = json.load(open(mp))
        except Exception:
            continue
        it = m.get("input_tokens", 0)
        ot = m.get("output_tokens", 0)
        tc = m.get("turn_count", 0)
        dr = m.get("documents_read", 0)
        # crude: input per turn ~ avg resident context; ratio in/out shows how
        # input-bound the task is.
        rows.append((it, ot, tc, dr, task))
    rows.sort(reverse=True)
    tot = sum(r[0] for r in rows)
    print(f"{'input_tok':>12} {'out_tok':>9} {'turns':>5} {'docs':>4} {'in/out':>6}  task")
    for it, ot, tc, dr, task in rows:
        ratio = (it / ot) if ot else 0
        print(f"{it:>12,} {ot:>9,} {tc:>5} {dr:>4} {ratio:>6.0f}  {task[:64]}")
    print(f"\nTOTAL input_tokens across {len(rows)} tasks: {tot:,}  (mean {tot//max(1,len(rows)):,})")


def breakdown(substr):
    cand = [d for d in glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT, "*", "iter0"))
            if substr in d]
    if not cand:
        print(f"no task dir matching '{substr}'")
        return
    tdir = cand[0]
    task = tdir.split("/")[-2]
    tpath = os.path.join(tdir, "transcript.jsonl")
    print(f"== {task} ==")
    # Track running estimate of resident "raw output" bytes (read/bash results).
    resident_chars = 0
    cum_in = 0
    print(f"{'turn':>4} {'in_tok':>9} {'cum_in':>11} {'tools':<22} {'resultlen':>10}")
    cur_turn = None
    pending = []  # (name, reslen) within a turn
    for line in open(tpath):
        d = json.loads(line)
        role = d.get("role")
        if role == "assistant":
            it = d.get("input_tokens") or 0
            cum_in += it
            tcs = d.get("tool_calls") or []
            names = ",".join(t["name"] for t in tcs)
            print(f"{d.get('turn'):>4} {it:>9,} {cum_in:>11,} {names:<22}")
        elif role == "tool":
            rl = len(d.get("result") or "")
            nm = d.get("tool_name", "")
            if rl > 2000:
                print(f"{'':>4} {'':>9} {'':>11} {'  -> '+nm:<22} {rl:>10,}")
    print(f"\ntotal input_tokens (sum per turn) = {cum_in:,}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) > 1:
        breakdown(sys.argv[1])
    else:
        rank_all()

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""ab_iters — free A/B of bare (iter0) vs an edited iter (iterN) across TRAIN.

turn_count) and grade.json (ens_pass_rate). This tool joins them so you can SEE,

Usage:
  ab_iters              # compare iter1 vs iter0 (default)

  ab_iters 2 1          # compare iter2 vs iter1

214	      ToolExecutor(documents_dir=..., output_dir=..., workspace_dir=..., ...)

250	        self.output_dir = self.sandbox.output_dir
251	        self.workspace_dir = self.sandbox.workspace_dir
252	        self.shell_timeout = shell_timeout
253	
254	        # Track usage for metrics.
255	        self.files_read: list[str] = []
256	        self.files_written: int = 0
257	        self.files_edited: int = 0
258	        self.bash_command_count: int = 0
259	        self.glob_count: int = 0

282	    def _resolve_read_path(self, path_str: str) -> str:
283	        """Resolve to a sandbox-relative path. Checks workspace, documents, output.
284	
285	        - Absolute sandbox paths (`/workspace/documents/...`) are validated and passed through.
286	        - Relative paths probe /workspace, /workspace/documents, /workspace/output in that
287	          order, falling back to /workspace/documents if nothing exists yet (matches
288	          legacy behavior).
289	        """
290	        if path_str.startswith("/"):
291	            Sandbox.assert_sandbox_path(path_str)
292	            return path_str
293	        # Relative — probe each mount.
294	        for mount in (WORKSPACE_PATH, DOCUMENTS_PATH, OUTPUT_PATH):
295	            candidate = f"{mount}/{path_str}"
296	            if self.sandbox.exists(candidate):
297	                return candidate
298	        # Default to documents (matches legacy fallback).
299	        return f"{DOCUMENTS_PATH}/{path_str}"
300	
301	    def _resolve_write_path(self, path_str: str) -> str:
302	        """Resolve to a sandbox-relative writable path.
303	
304	        - Absolute sandbox paths under /workspace/output or /workspace (excluding
305	          /workspace/documents) pass through.
306	        - Relative paths are written under /workspace/output.
307	        """
308	        if path_str.startswith("/"):
309	            Sandbox.assert_sandbox_path(path_str)
310	            if not Sandbox.is_writable(path_str):
311	                raise PermissionError(
312	                    f"write denied: {path_str} is read-only "
313	                    f"(documents) or outside /workspace"
314	                )
315	            return path_str
316	        return f"{OUTPUT_PATH}/{path_str}"

318	    def _resolve_search_path(self, path_str: str | None) -> str:
319	        """Resolve glob/grep search root to a sandbox-relative path."""
320	        if not path_str:
321	            return DOCUMENTS_PATH
322	        if path_str.startswith("/"):
323	            Sandbox.assert_sandbox_path(path_str)
324	            return path_str
325	        for mount in (DOCUMENTS_PATH, WORKSPACE_PATH, OUTPUT_PATH):
326	            candidate = f"{mount}/{path_str}"
327	            if self.sandbox.exists(candidate):
328	                return candidate
329	        return f"{DOCUMENTS_PATH}/{path_str}"

334	        """Execute a tool call and return the result as a string."""
335	        if isinstance(arguments, str):

396	    def _bash(self, command: str) -> str:
397	        if not command:
398	            return "Error: command is required"
399	
400	        self.bash_command_count += 1
401	        result = self.sandbox.exec(command, timeout=self.shell_timeout)
402	
403	        output = result.stdout
404	        if result.stderr:

407	            return f"Error: command timed out after {self.shell_timeout}s\n{output}"

409	            output += f"\n(exit code {result.returncode})"
410	        return output or "(no output)"

412	    def _read(self, file_path: str, offset: int | None, limit: int | None) -> str:
413	        if not file_path:
414	            return "Error: file_path is required"

416	        sb_path = self._resolve_read_path(file_path)
417	        if not self.sandbox.exists(sb_path):

422	            self.files_read.append(sb_path[len(DOCUMENTS_PATH) + 1:])
423	        else:

426	        content = self._read_and_parse(sb_path)

428	        if offset is not None or limit is not None:

436	    def _read_and_parse(self, sb_path: str) -> str:
437	        """Read content from a sandbox-relative path, parsing by extension.
438	
439	        For .docx, .pdf, .pptx, .xlsx the parsing happens *inside the
440	        sandbox* via `parse-doc <fmt> <sandbox-path>` (see
441	        sandbox/parsers/parse_doc.py). This keeps attacker-controlled
442	        document content from being parsed by host Python — pdfplumber /
443	        pandas / markitdown have a non-trivial vulnerability surface.
444	
445	        Plain text and everything else uses sandbox.read_file().
446	
447	        Parser failures (corrupt .docx, encrypted .pdf, etc.) come back as
448	        error strings so the agent can pivot to other documents rather
449	        than crashing the run.
450	        """
451	        suffix = Path(sb_path).suffix.lower()
452	        ext = suffix[1:]  # ".pdf" -> "pdf"
453	
454	        if ext in ("docx", "pdf", "pptx", "xlsx"):
455	            return self._parse_in_sandbox(ext, sb_path)
456	
457	        # Plain text (and everything else) — go through the sandbox.
458	        try:
459	            data = self.sandbox.read_file(sb_path)
460	            return data.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
461	        except IsADirectoryError:
462	            return f"Error: {sb_path} is a directory, not a file"
463	        except OSError as e:
464	            return f"Error: failed to read {sb_path}: {type(e).__name__}: {e}"
465	
466	    def _parse_in_sandbox(self, ext: str, sb_path: str) -> str:
467	        """Run the in-sandbox parser and return its stdout, or an error string."""
468	        # Quote the path for the shell — sandbox paths shouldn't contain
469	        # spaces or shell metas in practice, but defense in depth.
470	        result = self.sandbox.exec(
471	            f"parse-doc {ext} {shlex.quote(sb_path)}",
472	            timeout=120,  # large .pdfs / .xlsx can be slow
473	        )
474	        if result.timed_out:
475	            return f"Error: parser timed out on {sb_path} ({ext})"
476	        if result.returncode != 0:
477	            err = (result.stderr or "").strip().splitlines()
478	            tail = err[-1] if err else f"exit {result.returncode}"
479	            return f"Error: failed to parse {sb_path} ({ext}): {tail}"
480	        return result.stdout

482	    def _sandbox_to_host_path(self, sb_path: str) -> Path:
483	        """Map a sandbox-relative path back to the host filesystem.

519	            sb_path = None

528	        if not Sandbox.is_writable(sb_path):

551	    def _glob(self, pattern: str, search_path: str | None) -> str:

561	        # We need ordering by mtime — easiest to do that on the host since

arguments.command:
cat > /workspace/scratch/dup_analysis.py << 'PY'
import json, glob, os, hashlib, sys
from collections import defaultdict

ROOT = "/workspace/rollouts"

def analyze(task_dir, it="iter0"):
    tpath = os.path.join(task_dir, it, "transcript.jsonl")
    if not os.path.exists(tpath): return None
    task = os.path.basename(task_dir.rstrip("/"))
    # collect tool results in order
    reads = defaultdict(list)   # file_path -> list of (turn, len, hash)
    tool_results = []           # (turn, name, args, len, hash)
    for line in open(tpath):
        d = json.loads(line)
        if d.get("role") != "tool": continue
        res = d.get("result") or ""
        h = hashlib.md5(res.encode()).hexdigest()
        nm = d.get("tool_name","")
        args = d.get("arguments","")
        tool_results.append((d.get("turn"), nm, args, len(res), h))
    # find duplicate identical large results
    by_hash = defaultdict(list)
    for turn, nm, args, ln, h in tool_results:
        if ln >= 6000:
            by_hash[h].append((turn, nm, ln, args[:60]))
    dup_bytes = 0
    dup_groups = 0
    for h, occ in by_hash.items():
        if len(occ) > 1:
            dup_groups += 1
            ln = occ[0][2]
            dup_bytes += ln * (len(occ)-1)  # all but newest are redundant
    total_result_bytes = sum(ln for _,_,_,ln,_ in tool_results if ln>=6000)
    return task, len(tool_results), total_result_bytes, dup_groups, dup_bytes, by_hash

# rank all iter0 tasks by dup bytes
rows=[]
for td in sorted(glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT,"*",""))):
    if os.path.basename(td.rstrip("/"))=="_stats": continue
    r = analyze(td)
    if r: rows.append(r)
rows.sort(key=lambda x:-x[4])
print(f"{'task':52} {'#res':>5} {'bigbytes':>11} {'dupgrp':>6} {'dupbytes':>11} {'dup%':>5}")
for task,nres,tot,dg,db,_ in rows:
    pct = db/tot*100 if tot else 0
    print(f"{task[:52]:52} {nres:>5} {tot:>11,} {dg:>6} {db:>11,} {pct:>4.0f}%")

messages.content:
The cost structure is clear: context grows monotonically with distinct reads + accumulated drafts; system prompt is a fixed ~4.9K tok/turn. The committed eviction's −28% comes from read-then-draft giants. The **redline giants** (dev: 13M/12M/15M tasks that re-read clause-by-clause) likely revert to near-bare because the `limit=1` kill-switch disables eviction on the first re-read — that's the money left on the table.

Key realization: the per-doc **sticky** logic already bounds per-doc loops (each doc evicted+reread at most once). The global `limit=1` kill-switch is overly heavy-handed. Let me check how `run_harvey_agent` compares, then back up and design the experiment.

# Tool — `run_harvey_agent`

> The mutator invokes this from its shell to run a BATCH of Harvey rollouts concurrently, for triage.

## Purpose

Run the **frozen Harvey agent** on **one or many task ids** (a **batch**, run **concurrently**),
using your **current `harness/`**: the broker overlays your edited `system_prompt.md` / `tools.py` /
`agent_loop.py` onto the bare harvey-labs checkout and runs that (exactly what the standard eval
would run), producing a deliverable + trajectory **per task** you can inspect and proxy-grade. This
is how you *try* a harness edit before the canonical eval sees it — and how you buy **breadth**:
test one edit across several tasks in a **single call** (they run in parallel) instead of one slow
serial rollout at a time.

The Harvey agent is **frozen** here: this tool does not change the agent, the model, or the
endpoint — it executes the harness as-is. Your edits to `harness/` are what change the output.

## Interface

- **Input:** one or more `task_id`s from your **TRAIN access set** (the matters under `rollouts/` /
  the train manifest), space-separated: `run_harvey_agent <task_id> [task_id2 ...]`. The batch runs
  concurrently (capped at a few at a time). Held-out **val** ids are **not available**; a batch
  containing **any** non-train id is **refused whole**.
- **ASYNC — returns immediately, runs in the background.** A batch takes ~15-20 min (each rollout
  ~9-10 min), longer than the shell's 10-minute cap, so the command **detaches a worker that survives
  the cap** and returns at once. It prints a **done-marker path** `scratch/runs/.batch_<id>.done`
  (and a live log). **Workflow: launch → poll `test -f <marker>` → `cat` the marker for the summary →
  THEN `proxy_eval`.** The deliverables are NOT ready the instant it returns — polling first is
  required, and it's what prevents the old failure (client killed at 10 min, broker keeps charging,
  deliverables lost). Don't re-launch the same batch while it's still running — you'd pay twice.
- **Output (all under `scratch/`):** one dir per task — `scratch/runs/<task_id>/deliverable.md`
  (pass it to `proxy_eval`) **and `scratch/runs/<task_id>/turns.txt`** (the rollout's turn count) for
  **`check_runaway`** (the runaway gate) — both written when the batch completes. The done-marker
  holds the per-task summary (`wrote ... turns=...`, `skipped_for_budget`, or `OVER_BUDGET`); a task
  that errors is reported there but doesn't sink the rest of the batch.
- Writes **nothing** outside `scratch/`.

## Cost

≈ **~$1 per task** (one Harvey-agent rollout) × the number of tasks in the batch. **Metered** — see
`budget.md`; a batch is charged per task only up to the remaining cap (the excess is skipped, not
charged). The unit cost is surfaced by the `budget` tool so you can size your batch.

## Guardrails

- **Counts against the budget.** Charged per task, pre-spend. If the **whole** batch is over budget
  the call returns OVER_BUDGET (your "commit now" cue); a partially-over batch runs what fits and
  returns the rest as `skipped_for_budget`. Check `budget` to size your batch. The budget is there to
  be **used**: confirm a candidate across **several distinct train tasks in one batch** (and against
  bare on the same tasks), not a single rollout — breadth across different matters is what shows the
  edit *generalizes* to tasks you can't see, and it's the main thing to spend the budget on
  (CLAUDE.md §3). A near-empty ledger means you under-experimented.
- **Never writes to `rollouts/`.** `rollouts/` is the canonical standard-eval path, owned by the
  outer driver. This tool is private triage; its outputs are ephemeral `scratch/` artifacts and
  **must not feed selection**.
- **Train-only — the val set is unreachable.** Only TRAIN task ids resolve; the held-out val
  tasks are never mounted into the workspace and any non-train id is refused. Running the agent on
  a val task would contaminate the held-out verdict, so it is impossible by construction.
- Frozen agent: do not use this to "grade your own homework" — pair it with `proxy_eval` for a
  cheap private signal only, never as the verdict.

messages.content: Let me detect which train tasks actually hit the kill-switch in iter2 (eviction fired AND a re-read occurred) — those are where raising the limit changes behavior and gives me train signal.

arguments.command:
cd /workspace
python3 << 'PY'
import json, glob, os, re
ROOT="/workspace/rollouts"
# Detect, per iter2 transcript: did an eviction stub appear in the prompt? and did the agent re-read a file after it was read once?
for td in sorted(glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT,"*",""))):
    task=os.path.basename(td.rstrip("/"))
    if task=="_stats": continue
    tp=os.path.join(td,"iter2","transcript.jsonl")
    if not os.path.exists(tp): continue
    reads={}  # basename -> count
    stub_seen=False
    turns=0
    for line in open(tp):
        d=json.loads(line)
        r=d.get("role")
        if r=="assistant":
            turns=max(turns, d.get("turn") or 0)
        if r=="tool" and d.get("tool_name")=="read":
            args=d.get("arguments","")
            m=re.search(r'file_path["\s:]+([^",}]+)', args)
            if m:
                base=m.group(1).strip().rstrip("/").rsplit("/",1)[-1]
                reads[base]=reads.get(base,0)+1
        # stub marker would appear in tool result_preview/result of a read that was elided? No—stub replaces in prompt, not in transcript result.
        # Detect via assistant text mentioning context-saver? Not reliable.
    rereads={k:v for k,v in reads.items() if v>1}
    if rereads:
        print(f"{task[:55]:55} turns={turns:>3} REREADS={rereads}")
PY

messages.content:
Key finding: train re-reads are mostly of the agent's **own output deliverables** on **sub-28-turn tasks** (under the MIN_TURN gate, so eviction never fired). Only 2 train tasks cross MIN_TURN, and they re-read their own drafts near the end. So the train pool barely exercises the redline-giant re-fetch pattern — raising the global kill-switch is exactly the risky direction memory warns thrashed, and I can't validate it on train.

Let me examine the system prompt — a fixed per-turn cost paid on *every* task including sub-gate ones — to see if there's a safe, broad lever there.

# Tool — `dev_eval`

> Your **commit gate**: score your current `harness/` on the held-out-from-train **DEV set**, graded
> by the **ensemble** judge — the same grader the canonical verdict uses.

## Purpose

`dev_eval` runs the frozen Harvey agent on each **DEV** task using your **current `harness/`**, then
grades each deliverable with the **ensemble judge** (gpt-5-mini + gpt-5, escalating disagreements to
Sonnet). It returns **all three parts of the gate**: the **accuracy** (`dev_score` = mean ensemble
pass-rate), the **cost** (`cost.mean_input_tokens` / `mean_total_tokens` / `mean_turns`, plus per-task
`input_tokens` / `turns`), and a **per-task THRASH check** — so one call tells you whether accuracy
held, cost dropped, AND no single task blew up under the mean.

This is the signal you should **commit on**. It differs from `proxy_eval` in the two ways that matter:

| | `proxy_eval` | `dev_eval` |
|---|---|---|
| tasks | a TRAIN task you tune on | held-out DEV tasks you never tune on |
| grader | single cheap gpt-5-mini | **ensemble** (same as the real verdict) |
| use | EXPLORE — rank your own attempts | **DECIDE** — gate the commit |

The proxy is a fast, biased hint; the ensemble on held-out dev is measured the way the verdict is.
A high proxy score that does **not** hold up on `dev_eval` is exactly the signal to **not** commit.

## Interface

- **Input:** nothing = the **full** dev set (this is the gate — run it this way). A space-separated
  subset of **dev** ids is allowed (`dev_eval [task_id ...]`) but is only a **noisy, small-sample**
  read: it prints a `PARTIAL DEV … SMALL-SAMPLE` banner and must NOT be the basis for a commit (see
  Guardrails). Every id must be a dev id; any non-dev id (including any val id) refuses the whole call.
  The held-out val set is unreachable here.
- **ASYNC — returns immediately, runs in the background.** A full dev eval is ~30 rollouts
  (~15-25 min), longer than the shell's 10-minute cap, so the command detaches a worker that
  survives the cap and prints a **done-marker path** `scratch/runs/.dev_<id>.done`.
  **Workflow: launch → poll `test -f <marker>` → `cat` it for `dev_score` → commit (or not).**
- **Output** (the marker, read top to bottom):
  - a `PARTIAL DEV … SMALL-SAMPLE` banner **iff** you ran a subset — a heads-up that this read is a
    likely-overfit fluke, not a commit signal;
  - `dev_score=<accuracy>` over `n_graded/n_requested` tasks — the **accuracy gate**;
  - a `cost:` line (`mean_input_tokens` / `mean_total_tokens` / `mean_turns`) — the **cost objective**;
  - a `COMMIT only if ALL THREE hold` line (accuracy ≥ bare−noise AND cost clearly below bare AND no
    per-task THRASH) — the decision rule, restated inline;
  - a `THRASH CHECK:` block — per dev task, vs the per-task bare dev baseline. **THRASH** = a re-fetch
    **LOOP** (turns **≥2×** with tokens **≥1.5×**, or tokens **≥3×** with turns **≥1.5×** — a HARD reject); a single-axis rise flagged **≥1.5×** is a **watch**,
    including a **tokens-only ≥2× with turns ~flat** (a one-time re-read or GLM variance — weigh, don't
    auto-reject). A task with no bare baseline is reported **cost UNKNOWN** (never silently counted clean).
  - per-task lines (`acc=`, `in_tok=`, `turns=`). The full structured result is also written to
    `scratch/dev_eval_<id>.json`.

## Cost

≈ **~$1.10 per dev task** (one rollout ~$1 + one ensemble grade ~$0.10) × the number of dev tasks.
A full dev gate is ~30 tasks ≈ ~$33. **Metered** — charged per task up to the remaining cap; tasks
beyond the cap come back as `skipped_for_budget`. The gate is your **highest-value spend** (it is the
only held-out generalization signal), so budget for it: score only your **top 1–2** train-picked
candidates (CLAUDE.md §4), but score each on a **large** dev batch — the **full set is best**; a small
subset is a noisy read that overfits. Reserve enough cap to run at least **one full gate** as your
commit decision (and ideally a second, to re-gate after fixing a thrash). Reserve enough **wall-clock**
too — a full gate runs ~90 min, and `dev_eval` **refuses to start a full gate that can't finish

messages.content:
Decisive analysis complete. Key conclusions:
1. The committed iter2 read-eviction (−28%) is **banked** and concentrates on read-then-draft giants. On the dev **redline giants** (~40M tokens: subsequent/first-turn-redlines that re-reference docs), the `limit=1` global kill-switch disables eviction on the first backward-reference → those revert to near-bare. **That's the only sizable money left.**
2. The per-doc **sticky** logic already bounds *per-doc* loops (each doc evicted+reread at most once). So the global `limit=1` disable is overly heavy-handed — raising it lets eviction persist on the *cold tail* of redlines while sticky still prevents per-doc loops; the limit acts as a bounded backstop against re-read-everything flailing.
3. This is testable only on dev (redlines are dev-only), so I'll validate the edit is *wired correctly* on train, then spend one dev gate. Fallback on failure = banked iter2.

My candidate: a **single, attributable change** — `REFETCH_LIMIT 1→4` (keep MIN_TURN=28, WINDOW=12 so all proven read-then-draft behavior is untouched). Let me make the edit.

arguments.old_string:
# **Re-fetch kill-switch (thrash protector) — disable on the FIRST re-fetch.**
# A re-fetch of a previously-evicted output (a re-read of an evicted file, or a
# re-run of an evicted bash/grep/glob) means this task needs its outputs resident
# — exactly the clause-by-clause redline pattern. Once the run accumulates this
# many re-fetch events, eviction is DISABLED for the remainder of the run (the
# re-fetched output is also pinned sticky). Set to 1 so the VERY FIRST re-fetch
# turns eviction off: this bounds eviction's worst-case overhead to a single
# extra read (one re-orientation), making a re-fetch LOOP impossible by
# construction (a 4.4×-token dev blowup under the old limit=2 motivated this), and
# makes eviction self-targeting — redline tasks that re-read revert to bare almost
# immediately, while read-then-draft tasks that never re-read keep the full win.
_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT = int(os.environ.get("HARNESS_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT", "1"))
arguments.new_string:
# **Re-fetch backstop (thrash protector) — two layers, per-doc then global.**
# A re-fetch of a previously-evicted output (a re-read of an evicted file, or a
# re-run of an evicted bash/grep/glob) means the agent went BACK to a doc it had
# not touched for a full RECENT_WINDOW — the clause-by-clause redline pattern.
#   • PRIMARY protection is PER-DOC sticky (see `sticky_keys`): the very first
#     re-fetch of a given output pins THAT output resident forever, so it can never
#     be evicted again. A single document therefore cannot loop (evict→reread→evict)
#     — at most ONE extra read per distinct doc. The agent's "hot" working set
#     becomes resident after one re-read each; the genuinely cold tail keeps being
#     shed. This per-doc bound holds regardless of the global limit below.
#   • SECONDARY backstop is this GLOBAL limit: after this many *distinct* re-fetch
#     events across the whole run, eviction is disabled entirely (reverting to bare
#     for the rest of the run). It guards the pathological "re-read EVERYTHING"
#     flail (a many-small-docs extraction task that, once disrupted, re-reads all
#     its inputs) before it can re-inflate context.
# Why 4 (raised from 1): limit=1 disabled eviction on a redline's FIRST backward
# reference, so the cost-dominant redline GIANTS (subsequent/first-turn redlines,
# 13-15M input each) reverted to bare and captured ~no savings — the bulk of the
# remaining cost. With MIN_TURN=28 keeping the short/medium tasks (where the old
# limit=2 thrash lived, a 17→38-turn extraction at the then-MIN_TURN=20) entirely
# out of eviction, only true long runs reach here; per-doc sticky bounds each doc
# to one extra read, and the global=4 backstop bounds the cross-doc flail to ≤4
# wasted reads before reverting to bare. So eviction now persists on a redline's
# cold tail (the win) while staying bounded against a loop (the gate's THRASH check
# is the final guard — reject if any dev redline still loops).
_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT = int(os.environ.get("HARNESS_EVICT_REFETCH_LIMIT", "4"))

arguments.content:
# Lesson — raising the eviction REFETCH_LIMIT above 1 is a NET LOSER (train-confirmed, iter3)

**Iteration 3 hypothesis (rejected):** the committed iter2 eviction (`MIN_TURN=28, WINDOW=12,
REFETCH_LIMIT=1`) leaves the cost-dominant **redline giants** (dev-only: subsequent/first-turn-redline,
13-15M input each) near bare, because `limit=1` disables eviction on the redline's FIRST backward
doc-reference. I tried `REFETCH_LIMIT 1→4` (relying on the per-doc `sticky` to bound per-doc loops,
with the global limit as a backstop) to let eviction persist on a redline's **cold tail**.

**Result — REJECTED on the cheap train loop (no dev gate spent).** Batch `ac8b03a7` (5 long train tasks),
iter3(limit=4) vs bare / iter2:
- **markup-of-settlement-agreement: 2.75M (iter2) → 4.37M (limit=4)** = **+33% vs bare** (3.29M). turns 32→39.
- **intercreditor-agreement: 2.48M (iter2) → 4.03M (limit=4)** = **+44% vs bare** (2.80M). turns 21→36.
- motion-to-dismiss ~flat (5.27M→5.37M, 38→45t); the two sub-gate tasks (cim 19t, saas 20t) unchanged.
- Net over the 5: limit=4 **18.47M > iter2 17.55M > bare 18.06M** → worse than the committed harness AND bare.
- THRASH check "clean" (each <1.5× turns), but the **mean hid two real input regressions** — exactly the trap.

## Why (the mechanism, now verified twice)
Negotiation/redline/markup tasks **re-read** their inputs (position letters, settlement docs, the growing
deliverable). Eviction evicts an aged doc → the agent re-reads it → the full doc re-enters context. With
`limit=1` the first such re-read **disables** eviction early, so these re-readers stay ~bare (protected).
Raising the limit lets eviction keep **churning** the re-readers (evict→reread→evict across several docs),
re-inflating context turn after turn. **The dev redline giants ARE re-readers**, so they would balloon the
same way — there is no free redline capture via evict-then-reread. This is the same failure the iter2 memo
saw at `limit=2` ([[eviction-length-gate-and-refetch-killswitch]]); confirmed independently here at `limit=4`.

## Standing conclusion
`REFETCH_LIMIT=1` is a tuned local optimum, not a conservative guess — **do not raise it.** The committed
iter2 config (`MIN_TURN=28, WINDOW=12, MINCHARS=6000, SIZEGATE=250000, REFETCH_LIMIT=1`) is kept. iter3
committed **no change** (banked the validated iter2 −28% / dev_score 0.8171 ≥ bare 0.8079).

## Dead ends ruled out this iter (don't re-explore without a new idea)
- **Dedup of re-read duplicates:** ZERO exact-duplicate large tool results in ANY train transcript — the
  agent reads distinct docs, never re-emits identical bytes. No savings there.
- **System-prompt trim:** the editable `system_prompt.md` is only ~570 tok; the ~4,350-tok bulk is appended

messages.content:
Everything is clean and verified. The harness is in the validated iter2 committed state, all three files compile, and `agent_loop.py` is byte-identical to the iter2 backup.

## Iteration 3 — outcome

**Committed state: unchanged — the validated iter2 eviction is kept** (`MIN_TURN=28, WINDOW=12, REFETCH_LIMIT=1`; −28% input, dev_score 0.8171 ≥ bare 0.8079, 0 THRASH — verified by the prior full dev gate, and the files are diff-clean against that state).

**What I tried and why I rejected it.** The only sizable cost left is the dev **redline giants** (subsequent/first-turn-redlines, ~13–15M input each) where the `limit=1` kill-switch disables eviction on the first backward doc-reference → they revert to ~bare. I hypothesized that raising `REFETCH_LIMIT 1→4` (per-doc `sticky` bounds per-doc loops; global limit as backstop) would let eviction persist on their cold tail. I built it, unit-tested the logic, and probed it on a focused 5-task train batch ($5):

- **markup-settlement 2.75M→4.37M (+33% vs bare)**, **intercreditor 2.48M→4.03M (+44% vs bare)** — clear regressions.
- Net over the 5 tasks: `limit=4` (18.47M) > `iter2` (17.55M) > bare (18.06M) — **worse than the committed harness and bare.**

The mechanism is now confirmed twice (this batch + the prior `limit=2` memo): negotiation/redline tasks **re-read** their inputs, so eviction-with-rereads *churns* them instead of capturing them — and the dev redline giants **are** re-readers, so they'd balloon the same way. The THRASH check read "clean" only because the **mean hid** the two per-task input regressions — exactly the trap the manual warns about. There is no free redline capture via this lever.

**Decision discipline.** I deliberately did **not** spend my one remaining dev gate on a lever train had already disproven — that would re-confirm known iter2 numbers for ~$33 + 90 min with no upside. I reverted to the banked win and recorded the finding.

**Leverage banked for next iteration** (`.claude/memory/refetch-limit-raise-is-net-loser.md`): `REFETCH_LIMIT=1` is a tuned optimum, not a guess — don't raise it. Also ruled out: dedup-of-re-reads (zero duplicate large reads exist in any transcript), system-prompt trim (editable part only ~570 tok), and noted the remaining giant cost is structural (accumulated drafts + re-reading redlines). Plus a hazard note: the stale `scratch/test/harness/` copy (now deleted) hijacks `import harness`.

Budget used: **$5 / $160** (one train batch); ~151 min wall-clock to spare. The validated −28% cost win stands, with a verified lesson that narrows the search for future runs.
